**Mexican mint** is an herb that goes by several names, including **Cuban/Puerto Rican oregano**, **Indian borage/mint**, and **Spanish thyme**.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

The herb is believed to be native to South-eastern Africa but has spread worldwide.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3) Its toothed leaves are thick and succulent,[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4) with a flavor is similar to that of mint but also of thyme, sage, and oregano.[[1]](#cite_note-:2-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:0-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Store like other delicate fresh herbs.

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Mexican mint features in Caribbean cuisine, where it is used to season savory preparations like stews, sauces, and marinades.[[3]](#cite_note-:3-3)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5) It can also be used in Southeast Asian cuisines.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)[[5]](#cite_note-:1-5)

## Substitution

[edit | edit source]

If you cannot get access to the herb itself, try incorporating a mixture of thyme, oregano, and sage.[[4]](#cite_note-:4-4)

## Recipes

[edit | edit source]

Category *Recipes using Mexican mint* not found
